description:
Sombra is the Spanish word for shade, and in Ecuador that shade comes naturally. The country's persistent cloud cover filters sunlight before it reaches the tobacco plants, producing a lighter, more refined wrapper leaf without the artificial tenting used elsewhere. CAO built the Sombra around that leaf to complete the FASA trilogy, pairing the Ecuadorian Connecticut shade-grown wrapper with the same Cameroon binder and filler of Dominican Piloto Cubano and Nicaraguan leaf from Jalapa, Estelí, and Condega used throughout the line.
With the Sol made in Honduras, the Noche in Nicaragua, and the Sombra in the Dominican Republic, each entry in the trilogy also comes from a different factory and country of production. Released in 2025. Available in two sizes in 24-count boxes. Mild to medium in body. Made at the General Cigar Dominicana factory in the Dominican Republic.

The Arturo Fuente Hemingway series was among the first modern lines to reintroduce the classic Cuban perfecto shape to the American market at scale. Perfectos require considerably more skill to roll than straight vitolas and were largely absent from Dominican production until the Fuente family made them the foundation of this line. The series is named after Ernest Hemingway, whose spare, deliberate style the cigars are meant to evoke.
The Maduro variant swaps the signature African Cameroon wrapper of the standard Hemingway for a Connecticut Broadleaf Maduro, while keeping the same aged Dominican binder and filler underneath. The Broadleaf is a thick, naturally sweet leaf that ferments to a deep brown and contributes a heavier body and additional complexity compared to the Cameroon. The Maduro is released in limited quantities seasonally, generally around Father's Day and the holidays, making it considerably harder to find than the standard line. Handmade in the Dominican Republic. Medium strength.

Flavor_Profile: Cedar, Green Apple, Sassafras, Cream, Earth

description:
When CAO began searching for Brazilian wrapper tobacco at the tail end of the 1990s cigar boom, Brazilian leaf was not a priority for most growers and the quality was inconsistent. After five years of searching, they found what they were looking for in the Bahia region, a dark, oily Arapiraca wrapper that became the foundation of the Brazilia. Introduced in 2001, it was the first mass-produced cigar to use a Brazilian Arapiraca wrapper and is widely credited with sparking renewed industry interest in Brazilian leaf, as other manufacturers followed CAO into the Bahia region in the years that followed.
The blend pairs that Arapiraca wrapper with a Nicaraguan binder and aged Nicaraguan long-leaf filler. Rated 91 by Cigar Aficionado. Full in body. Made in Honduras.

The Partagás y Nada Más is a medium-bodied cigar that delivers refined character and classic Cuban-inspired flavor. True to its name, which means "Partagás and nothing more," this cigar focuses on balance, tradition, and pure tobacco enjoyment. Expect notes of earth, cedar, and black pepper with a hint of sweetness that lingers just enough to keep each draw interesting. Smooth in strength but rich in flavor, it is ideal for those who enjoy complexity without overwhelming intensity.
Perfect for seasoned smokers seeking subtlety or newcomers ready to explore the world of premium cigars. This is Partagás in its purest form.

The Gran Habano Habano No 3 is a medium-bodied cigar crafted with a unique blend of Nicaraguan, Mexican, and Costa Rican tobaccos. Wrapped in a smooth Habano leaf, it delivers a well-balanced profile with notes of cedar, spice, roasted coffee, and toasted nuts. Rich in flavor yet approachable, this cigar is a standout choice for smokers who appreciate complexity without overwhelming strength.
This year’s Father’s Day cigar is sweet, spicy, and just a little mischievous, just like Dad. Notes of vanilla, oak, and warm spice shine through, no matter which end you light.

Two masters, one blend. This cigar delivers a bold, refined profile with notes of toasted oak, baking spice, and a subtle molasses sweetness. It’s a meeting of minds and the result is nothing short of exceptional

The Dominicana is the newest entry in the Deadwood lineup and the most distinct from its sisters. Where the rest of the line is built around Nicaraguan tobacco and dark Maduro wrappers, the Dominicana takes the opposite approach: an Ecuadorian Connecticut shade wrapper over a Mexican San Andrés binder, with Dominican long-leaf filler sourced from José Mendez & Co. It is the first Drew Estate cigar made in Nicaragua to focus primarily on Dominican tobacco, and the mildest of the Deadwood blends by a considerable margin. Sweetened cap, four sizes. Mild to medium in body. Made at La Gran Fabrica Drew Estate in Estelí, Nicaragua.

description: The latest addition to Hoyo's core lineup, the Shade, is a Honduran puro crafted entirely from Honduran tobaccos. It features a shade grown wrapper cultivated in Honduras, paired with Honduran binder and filler, delivering a mellow to medium-bodied profile.

To mark three decades of craftsmanship, Litto and Ines Gomez have introduced the La Flor Dominicana 30th Anniversary cigar, a limited-edition release that embodies the brand's dedication to quality and innovation. Handcrafted at Tabacalera La Flor S.A. in the Dominican Republic, this cigar features an Ecuadorian Sumatra wrapper, a rare aged Dominican binder from Cotuí, and fillers from La Canela. 

Measuring 6 ½ x 58, the cigar is presented in LFD's signature box-pressed Chisel shape, a unique wedge-shaped figurado. Each cigar is adorned with three bands, including a footband marked "30 Years" and a main band featuring a pink tobacco flower, symbolizing the company's name, "The Dominican Flower."

Limited to only 2,000 humidors, each containing 60 cigars, this release is a collector's item. The humidors themselves were crafted at La Flor Dominicana's box factory in the Dominican Republic, featuring an image of Litto and Ines on horseback in one of their tobacco fields.

The Foundation Aksum Maduro is a medium to full-bodied cigar crafted by Nick Melillo of Foundation Cigars. This blend features a dark Ecuadorian Sumatra-seed wrapper, a USA Connecticut Broadleaf binder, and Nicaraguan fillers from Estelí and Jalapa. Handmade at AJ Fernandez’s Tabacalera Fernandez factory in Nicaragua, the Aksum Maduro offers a refined smoking experience.
Previously known as Metapa, the Aksum Maduro pays homage to the ancient Aksumite Kingdom, believed to be the resting place of the Ark of the Covenant. The cigar’s band features an image of Ras Makonnen, father of Haile Selassie, aligning with Foundation's tradition of incorporating historical and cultural themes into their branding.

Filler: Nicaraguan, Dominican, US Broadleaf, Mexican San Andres
description: The Chillin' Moose by Foundry Tobacco Co. is a medium-bodied cigar that offers a complex blend of tobaccos from five countries. It features a Connecticut Habano Rosado wrapper, an Ecuadorian Sumatra binder, and a filler blend of Nicaraguan, Dominican, U.S. Broadleaf, and Mexican San Andrés tobaccos

The Medulla Maduro takes everything people loved about the original and wraps it in a rich San Andrés Maduro leaf. Rolled at CLE’s factory in Honduras, this cigar stays true to its roots with bold Honduran Corojo filler and binder. The maduro wrapper adds a deeper sweetness and a touch of spice, bringing out notes of dark chocolate, leather, espresso, earth, and black pepper.

The Jamastran Valley in Honduras is where the Eiroa family has grown tobacco for generations, and this cigar is a direct tribute to that ground. Christian Eiroa described it at release as a return to the bolder, fuller style he built his reputation on during his years at Camacho. The blend is a Honduran puro from wrapper to filler, using high-priming Corojo leaf throughout, the upper primings of the plant that receive the most sun and produce the darkest, most concentrated tobacco. Both sizes come in trapezoid-shaped boxes of 18, a design Eiroa favors for aging. The 11/18 vitola was the 2019 TAA exclusive. Debuted at the 2019 TAA convention. Medium to full in body. Made at the El Aladino factory in Danlí, Honduras.

A rare annual release from Arturo Fuente’s Unnamed Reserve series, the 2021 edition comes in a 5 ¾ by 48 corona gorda that was rolled in 2016 and released five years later. It features a sun grown Rosado wrapper over Dominican binder and filler, all produced at Tabacalera A. Fuente y Cia. Only 1,000 boxes of 20 were made.
This cigar shows off classic Fuente craftsmanship with a smooth draw, even burn, and refined construction. Each stick comes in cellophane and a cedar sleeve marked ProduXion 2016, nodding to the early aging that sets this release apart.
Ideal for collectors or anyone who appreciates pedigree, patience, and a quiet sense of excellence.

The fourth release in the Essence Series, the Essence Connecticut brings a mellow and elegant character to the E.P. Carrillo lineup. It features an Ecuadorian Connecticut wrapper, a Honduran Criollo 98 binder, and a blend of Honduran and Dominican fillers. The result is a smooth and balanced cigar with notes of cream, hay, light pepper, and subtle almond.
Rolled at Agroindustrias Diadema in Honduras, it offers a refined draw and clean finish that make it a perfect pick for those who enjoy Connecticut cigars with a little more depth. Available in Robusto, and Toro sizes, each in 24-count boxes.

The E.P. Carrillo Endure is a return to tradition with a rare Meerapfel-grown Cameroon wrapper that hasn’t been featured in a Perez-Carrillo blend for decades. This is a cigar built on legacy, combining a smooth and flavorful exterior with a Nicaraguan core that brings both strength and finesse.
Medium to full in body, it offers natural sweetness, floral touches, baking spice, and earthy depth, rounded out by a peppery finish and just a hint of nuttiness. Each puff tells a story of resilience and craftsmanship.
Rolled at Tabacalera La Alianza in the Dominican Republic, the Endure is available in box-pressed Robusto and Toro sizes, both packaged in 10-count boxes. A Corona Gorda is set to join the line soon.

Volition America by Gurkha is a patriotic cigar line crafted in partnership with the Folds of Honor Foundation. Each 6x54 Toro comes in one of three blends—Maduro, Habano, or Connecticut—offering options from bold to mild. The cigars are made in Nicaragua with a Nicaraguan binder and a mix of Nicaraguan and Dominican fillers. Every box of 13 cigars symbolizes the 13 folds of the American flag, and 13% of proceeds go toward educational scholarships for families of fallen or disabled military members and first responders.

Every box of 13 cigars symbolizes the 13 folds of the American flag, and 13% of proceeds go toward educational scholarships for families of fallen or disabled military members and first responders.

Marking ten years since the original New World debuted, the Decenio is A.J. Fernandez’s way of celebrating the line that helped put his name on the map. Made at the San Lotano factory in Ocotál, Nicaragua, this anniversary blend brings refinement without losing the punch that New World fans expect.
The flavor profile leans rich and savory. Expect notes of dark chocolate, espresso, and black pepper, layered over hints of mesquite and a tangy sweetness that lingers. The result is a balanced, complex smoke that stays interesting from first light to final inch.

Filler: Nicaraguan Esteli, Honduran La Entrada, Dominican Piloto & Andullo, Cyprian Latakia
description:
The Stokk is the fourth entry in CAO's Arcana series, developed in collaboration with Peter Stokkebye, the Danish pipe tobacco house, both of which are owned by Scandinavian Tobacco Group. The collaboration is built around a concept that has no real precedent in handmade cigars: the filler is divided into two distinct sections, each loaded with a different rare tobacco, so that the character of the cigar deliberately shifts halfway through.
The first half burns through Dominican andullo, the same densely fermented, palm-pod-aged tobacco introduced in the Mortal Coil. At the midpoint, the flame reaches Cyprian Latakia, a fire-cured tobacco from Cyprus that is a cornerstone of English-style pipe tobacco blends and appears here in a premium handmade cigar. Honduran tobacco rounds out the filler in both halves. The wrapper is a US Broadleaf over a Nicaraguan binder. The wooden boxes are finished with rune-like markings in tribute to Stokkebye's Scandinavian roots. Released August 2025, limited to 3,500 boxes of 20. Available in a single belicoso. Made at the STG Estelí factory in Nicaragua.

Crafted in collaboration with A.J. Fernandez, Wabi Sabi is a limited-edition offshoot of the original Kintsugi line. It’s rolled in Nicaragua and offered in a single 6 x 52 toro,  boxed in runs of just 1,500 so when it’s gone, it’s gone. Inspired by the Japanese concept of Wabi Sabi, finding beauty in imperfection, this cigar isn’t about chasing perfection. It’s about honoring character, balance, and craftsmanship.
A worthy addition for collectors and regulars alike.

This one’s your invitation to pause and soak in a moment of calm. Handmade at La Isla in the Dominican Republic, Do Not Disturb opens smooth and grounded. Think earthy notes, fresh hay, and a whisper of tea, with a gentle spice that grows on you as it goes. It evolves steadily, rewarding quiet moments with thoughtful flavor.

The CAO Surplus Shade delivers a smooth, budget-friendly smoke wrapped in a silky Honduran Connecticut leaf over an Ecuadorian Sumatra binder and Dominican Olor long-fillers. Balanced and creamy with just enough flavor to keep things interesting, it’s a dependable everyday cigar that proves great value doesn’t have to mean cutting corners.

The CAO Surplus Maduro delivers bold flavor at a budget-friendly price, featuring a dark Mexican San Andrés wrapper over an Ecuadorian Sumatra binder and Dominican Olor long-fillers. Medium-bodied with rich cocoa and spice notes, it’s a satisfying everyday cigar that proves big taste doesn’t have to break the bank.

The CAO Surplus Sweet combines an Ecuadorian Sumatran wrapper with an Ecuadorian Sumatra binder and Dominican Olor long-fillers, topped with a sweetened cap. Mild to medium-bodied with a smooth, approachable flavor, it’s an easygoing everyday smoke with a hint of sweetness to keep things interesting.

Filler: Dominican Piloto, Honduran La Entrada and Jamastran, Nicaraguan
description:
The Cohiba Rubicon arrived in August 2025 as the most accessible entry in the non-Cuban Cohiba lineup, priced under ten dollars across all three sizes. It sits at a deliberate remove from something like the Spectre, which runs $150 a cigar, and the name carries the weight of that contrast: crossing the Rubicon, the point of no return.
The blend puts an Ecuadorian Habano wrapper over a Connecticut Broadleaf binder, with filler from four growing regions across three countries: Dominican Piloto Cubano, Honduran leaf from La Entrada and the Jamastran Valley, and Nicaraguan leaf from Estelí. Six tobaccos in total. All three sizes are box pressed, offered in 20-count boxes. Medium in body. Made at the HATSA factory in Danlí, Honduras.

The Alec Bradley Chunk Maduro is a medium-bodied powerhouse rolled at the HATSA factory in Honduras. It pairs a dark Mexican Maduro wrapper with a Honduran Jamastrán binder and a blend of Dominican Piloto Cubano and Nicaraguan tobaccos. Expect waves of cocoa, molasses, cedar, nutty undertones, and a touch of pepper in every rich, satisfying draw.

The Ecuadorian Habano wrapper is the defining element of this blend. Grown in the nitrogen-rich volcanic soils of the Andes foothills from Havana seed, it is a leaf with a distinct texture, color, and character that sets it apart from most other wrapper varieties on the market. Camacho built the Ecuador around it, pairing the wrapper with a Brazilian Mata Fina binder and a filler combining high-priming Honduran leaf and Dominican Pelo de Oro Ligero. Rated 91 by Cigar Aficionado. Medium to full in body. Made at the Diadema Cigars de Honduras factory in Danlí, Honduras.

Camacho released the Broadleaf in September 2023, and the wrapper is its defining feature. Broadleaf tobacco has long been associated with the Connecticut River Valley, but Camacho's master blenders grew this leaf in Honduras, making it the first Honduran Broadleaf wrapper in the brand's portfolio. The result is dark, oily, and toothy, with the natural sweetness the leaf is known for carrying alongside the earthiness of Jamastran Valley soil. The filler draws from Honduras and the Dominican Republic, bound in a Honduran binder. Rated 91 by Cigar Aficionado. Available in three sizes, all in 20-count boxes. Medium to full in body. Made at the Diadema Cigars de Honduras factory in Danlí.

Alec Bradley Chunk Shade brings that stout, chunky format you know and love in the Chunk family but with a smoother side. It’s wrapped in Honduran grown Connecticut Shade, built with a U.S. Broadleaf binder, and filled with a balanced blend of Dominican, Honduran, and Nicaraguan tobaccos made at the HATSA factory in Honduras. It’s easygoing yet bold, perfect for kicking back at any time.

The CAO Gold was among the first cigars the brand released when founder Cano Ozgener expanded from pipe tobacco into premium handmades, and it remains one of the longest-running blends in the CAO portfolio. Where most of the CAO catalog leans toward bold and adventurous, the Gold is the line's deliberate counterpoint, built for consistency and approachability rather than intensity.
The wrapper is an Ecuadorian Connecticut seed shade-grown leaf, golden and silky, over a Cuban-seed Nicaraguan binder and long-leaf Nicaraguan filler. Rated 91 by Cigar Aficionado on multiple occasions. Mild to medium in body. Made in Nicaragua.

The Zócalo takes its name from the central public square found in Mexican cities, and it marked a first for CAO: the brand had never used a Mexican wrapper in its nearly 25 years on the market before this blend debuted in 2018. The wrapper is a Morron varietal grown in the San Andrés Valley, a leaf that carries more natural sweetness and less of the coarse character associated with other Mexican varietals. The blend pairs that wrapper with a Cameroon binder and Nicaraguan long-leaf filler. Originally released as a single limited-edition size, it returned in 2021 as a regular production line in three sizes. Medium to full in body. Made at the STG Estelí factory in Nicaragua.

Cuban seed tobacco has been growing in Colombia since the early nineteenth century, but the leaf rarely made it into premium cigars. The variety CAO built this blend around is called Ica Mazinga, cultivated in the Montes de Maria mountains near the Colombian Caribbean coast, and it took roughly fifteen years of development before master blender Rick Rodriguez considered it ready. CAO Colombia was released in 2014 as part of the brand's World series, the first cigar to feature Ica Mazinga as a primary component.
The filler pairs that Colombian leaf with Brazilian Mata Fina, bound in a Cameroon binder and finished in a Nicaraguan Jamastran Rosado wrapper. Rated 92 by Cigar Aficionado and named their Best Buy under six dollars in 2015. Medium in body. Made in Nicaragua.

The Moontrance sits in the CAO Flavours line alongside the Cherrybomb, sharing the same base construction: a Cameroon wrapper over Dominican binder and long-leaf filler. The infusion combines bourbon vanilla, Georgia peach nectar, natural exotic fruit extracts, and a touch of Hawaiian white honey. Available in cigarillo, petit corona, and corona sizes. Mild in body. Made in the Dominican Republic.

The Cherrybomb is part of the CAO Flavours line, a collection of infused cigars built on the same quality construction standards as the rest of the CAO portfolio. The base blend is a Cameroon wrapper over a Connecticut Broadleaf binder and aged Dominican long-leaf filler. Cherry and black currant infusions are applied to the tobacco, with a creamy vanilla note underneath. Available in a small cigarillo size in aluminum tubes and a full corona. Mild in body. Made in the Dominican Republic.

rturo Fuente Exquisitos Maduro is one of the quiet workhorses of the Fuente portfolio, a small cigar with a long family history behind it. Rolled in the Dominican Republic, it represents the classic Fuente approach to craftsmanship in a size that has been popular for decades with smokers who want something quick without sacrificing quality. The line has been around long enough to earn a loyal following, especially among fans of traditional handmade cigars.
The Maduro version uses a dark wrapper that gives the cigar its signature look and adds a touch of old world character. Exquisitos Maduro is known for consistent construction, dependable draw, and the kind of everyday reliability that has kept Fuente at the center of the premium cigar world for generations. It remains a staple for anyone who appreciates a shorter handmade cigar with a pedigree.

Macanudo Emissary France is a premium release designed to highlight rare French-grown tobacco from the Mugron River Valley, between Bordeaux and the Spanish border. The line is a part of Macanudo’s Emissary sub-brand, which focuses on unique European leaf and blends offering elevated complexity. 
Blended from tobaccos aged four to ten years and backed by craftsmanship from the STG Danlí factory in Honduras, Emissary France uses a 10-year aged Ecuadorian Habano wrapper, a 10-year aged U.S. Broadleaf binder, and filler leaves including Dominican Piloto, U.S. Broadleaf, Brazilian Arapiraca and the French leaf that gives the cigar its distinction.
Emissary France is positioned in the medium to medium-full strength range, offering an experience that appeals to seasoned smokers who appreciate thoughtful blending and nuanced leaf origins.

La Flor Dominicana Cabinet Oscuro Natural comes from the period when Litto Gomez was shaping LFD into a serious Dominican brand with its own voice. The Cabinet series was meant to show what well aged Dominican tobacco could do when treated with patience and restraint. It carried a richer profile than most Dominican cigars of its time, but it wasn’t built to overpower. Instead, it highlighted the depth and character of the tobaccos Gomez was cultivating as he developed the identity that would eventually define LFD.
The blend uses Dominican filler and binder with a naturally dark, oily wrapper that gives the cigar its name. Cabinet Oscuro Natural has stayed in circulation for years because it represents an early chapter in LFD’s evolution, before the rise of the full strength Ligero lines. It shows the brand’s roots in careful fermentation, consistent construction, and the desire to push Dominican tobacco toward new expressions. For many smokers, it remains one of the blends that explains how LFD became the company it is today.

La Flor Dominicana Double Ligero Digger Maduro comes from the era when LFD was redefining what full strength Dominican cigars could be. Litto Gomez built the Double Ligero line to showcase the raw power and depth of his estate-grown Dominican ligero, and the Digger became the oversized symbol of that philosophy. It was designed for smokers who wanted a long, unapologetically strong cigar and a clear example of how far Dominican tobacco could be pushed without losing structure or complexity.
The Maduro version uses a dark, well-fermented wrapper that adds extra richness to the already bold blend. Despite its size and nicotine strength, the Digger Maduro has earned a loyal following because it delivers what the Double Ligero line promised from the start: a showcase of Dominican potency backed by solid construction and slow, steady combustion. It remains one of LFD’s most recognizable cigars and a touchstone for anyone exploring the stronger side of modern Dominican blending.

La Flor Dominicana Double Ligero Digger Natural is part of the chapter where Litto Gomez pushed Dominican tobaccos into territory few brands were exploring at the time. The Double Ligero line was built to highlight strength, showing what could be done with heavy doses of estate-grown ligero from the La Canela farm. The Digger became the outsized expression of that idea, a cigar created for smokers who wanted both intensity and an extended smoking session.
The Natural version uses a well aged sun-grown wrapper that keeps the focus on the core ligero-driven blend. It shows the raw power and earthy depth that made the Double Ligero series a landmark for full strength Dominican cigars, while still carrying the steady burn and robust construction LFD built its name on. The Digger Natural remains one of the clearest examples of how LFD blended boldness with craftsmanship during its rise.

La Flor Dominicana Double Ligero Chisel Natural represents one of the most notable moments in modern cigar design. The Chisel shape was created by Litto Gomez and became the first cigar shape to receive a United States patent, showing how LFD was experimenting with form at the same time it was redefining strength. Paired with the Double Ligero blend, the Chisel demonstrated how focused airflow and concentrated smoke output could amplify the character of Dominican ligero grown at the La Canela farm.
The Natural version uses a sun grown wrapper that keeps the spotlight on the core Dominican tobaccos that built the Double Ligero name. It reflects the original idea behind the line, which was to show the power and depth that carefully cultivated ligero can offer while maintaining solid construction and a steady burn. The Chisel Natural remains one of LFD’s signature cigars because it captures both the creativity and the intensity that shaped the brand’s early rise.

Avo Uvezian spent his life finding harmony between disparate elements, whether in music or in tobacco. The Syncro series, which takes its name from the musical concept of synchronization, applied that philosophy directly to blending, each entry drawing tobaccos from multiple origins and marrying them into a single expression. Ritmo, the Spanish word for rhythm, is the third entry in the series and the most ambitious in scope.
The blend draws from seven countries across two continents. The filler combines tobaccos from Nicaragua, Honduras, Peru, Brazil, and the Dominican Republic. A Mexican binder adds spice and earthiness to the core. The wrapper is an Ecuadorian habano leaf that ties the whole construction together. All vitolas are box pressed. The Ritmo was the first AVO release following Avo Uvezian's death in March 2017 at the age of 91, and carries that weight with it. Produced at the Oettinger Kelner Cigars factory in the Dominican Republic.

Avo Uvezian was born in Beirut in 1926, trained at Juilliard, and spent decades as a jazz pianist and composer. He co-wrote Strangers in the Night, the song most associated with Frank Sinatra, and spent years performing nightly at his restaurant in Puerto Rico while smoking custom blended cigars he kept on top of the piano. Those cigars, originally sold under the name Bolero in San Juan, were made by master blender Hendrik Kelner. The two men formalized their partnership in 1987, changed the name to Avo, and launched the brand at the Davidoff shop in New York City. Davidoff acquired distribution rights in 1995. Uvezian died in 2017 at the age of 91.
The Classic is the foundational blend of the line and remains its most widely recognized expression. The wrapper is an Ecuadorian Connecticut shade grown leaf, a blond, silky tobacco grown under cloth tents that produces a mild, creamy character. The binder and filler are Dominican long filler tobaccos blended by Kelner. The overall profile is mild to medium with a smooth, consistent character that has made the Classic one of the most reliable everyday cigars in the premium Dominican category for nearly four decades. Rated 90 by Cigar Aficionado. Produced at the Oettinger Kelner Cigars factory in the Dominican Republic.

Alec Bradley Black Market is one of the cigars that pushed the brand into wider recognition in the early 2010s. Released during a period when Alec Bradley was experimenting with bolder, less traditional profiles, Black Market stood out with its unusual banding, darker aesthetic, and willingness to showcase heavier tobaccos. It quickly became a staple in humidors across the country and helped solidify Alec Bradley as a company willing to take risks at a time when many brands were staying on familiar ground.
The blend uses a Nicaraguan wrapper over a Honduran binder with filler from Panama and Honduras. The inclusion of Panamanian leaf is part of what made this cigar notable, since that region is rarely used in premium blends and adds a distinct character that set Black Market apart from its competitors. The cigar has remained a long running line in the Alec Bradley portfolio because it represents a turning point for the company, showing how creative sourcing and a willingness to break from tradition could earn a lasting place in the market.

Sarzedas is a true revival. The name dates back to 1900, when JC Newman first rolled the line and marketed it as “The Aromatic Cigar.” The company reintroduced it in 2025, once again tying modern production to its early history. Rolled at JC Newman’s PENSA factory in Estelí, Nicaragua, Sarzedas bridges the company’s Tampa roots with the craftsmanship of its contemporary Nicaraguan operation.
The blend was designed as a flavorful, balanced cigar rather than a niche project. Created by Rich Dolak, JC Newman’s longtime operations vice president, it uses an Ecuadorian Shade wrapper over Dominican and Nicaraguan binder and filler. The result is a smooth, aromatic cigar that honors the brand’s origins while offering the refinement and consistency expected from JC Newman today.

La Aroma de Cuba Mi Amor is one of the cornerstone collaborations between José “Pepin” Garcia and Ashton, created to give the La Aroma line a bolder, more modern identity. Produced at the My Father factory in Estelí, Nicaragua, it marked the moment when the brand shifted from its Cuban heritage revival toward being recognized for serious Nicaraguan craftsmanship. The name Mi Amor, meaning “my love,” reflects both the personal pride behind the project and the passion that defines Garcia’s blending style.
The cigar uses a dark Mexican San Andrés wrapper over Nicaraguan binder and filler, creating a rich yet balanced profile that helped make it one of the most awarded cigars in the La Aroma portfolio. Mi Amor remains a defining example of how the partnership between Ashton and the Garcias successfully combined heritage branding with the power and depth of Nicaraguan tobacco. It continues to stand as one of the most respected lines in modern premium cigar making.

Flavor_Profile: Dark Chocolate, Espresso, Cinnamon, Earth, Pepper
description:
Christian Luis Eiroa launched CLE Cigar Company in 2012, and when June 2020 marked his 25th year in the industry, he broke a promise he had made to himself never to release an anniversary or limited cigar.
The 25th Anniversary is a Honduran puro from wrapper to filler, built entirely from Authentic Corojo grown on the Eiroa family farms. Different primings and vintages of that leaf are combined across filler, binder, and wrapper to build complexity within a single tobacco. All sizes are box pressed and presented in 25-count boxes. The 11/18, a double-tapered vitola Eiroa introduced during his Camacho years and carried into CLE, was released as a 2020 TAA exclusive. Made at the El Aladino factory in Honduras.

The Eiroa family's roots in tobacco trace to 1916, when Christian Eiroa's great grandfather Generoso took work with the Cuban Land and Leaf Tobacco Company. Four generations later, Christian launched the Eiroa Classic at the 2013 IPCPR trade show alongside the opening of his El Aladino factory in Danlí, naming the cigar after his family as a statement of confidence in the blend. It is a Honduran puro from wrapper to filler, built entirely from Authentic Corojo grown on the Eiroa family farms in the Jamastran Valley. The wood boxes the cigars are packaged in, fitted with a leather pull and a removable lid, are a tribute to Christian's grandfather, who kept his cigars in a trunk at the foot of his bed. Medium to full in body. Made at the El Aladino factory in Danlí, Honduras.

Flavor_Profile: Dark Chocolate, Espresso, Spice, Nuts, Earth
description:
Eiroa The First 20 Years Maduro marks a milestone in Christian Eiroa’s career, celebrating two decades of his work in premium cigar making. The line honors both his family’s long history in Honduras and his personal evolution as a blender. Built entirely from Honduran tobaccos grown on the Eiroa family’s farms, it reflects the pride of a brand that continues to champion authentic Corojo cultivation and the craftsmanship that has defined the Eiroa name for generations.
The Maduro version uses a dark, well-fermented Honduran wrapper that deepens the blend’s natural sweetness and adds a layer of richness without overwhelming the palate. Construction is tight, the draw is deliberate, and the flavor progression showcases the complexity of properly aged Corojo leaf. Eiroa The First 20 Years Maduro stands as both a personal statement from its creator and a benchmark for what modern Honduran cigars can achieve when tradition and precision meet.

The Punch Egg Roll XL is the supersized return of a cigar that earned its following the honest way: it was good enough that people kept asking for more. The original Egg Roll debuted in 2019 as part of Punch's ongoing series of food-themed limited releases, a lineage that includes Chop Suey, Kung Pow!, and Spring Roll. The XL keeps the same blend intact, a Connecticut Broadleaf wrapper over an Ecuadorian Sumatra binder with fillers from Colombia, Mexico, the Dominican Republic, and the United States, but bumps the size from a 4.5 x 50 to a full 6 x 60 Gigante, complete with the shaggy foot that became part of the original's identity. Rolled at General Cigar's HATSA factory in Danlí, Honduras, it arrives packaged in a Chinese takeout container as a nod to the 2026 Lunar New Year and the Year of the Horse. At under nine dollars a stick, it punches well above its price point and won't be around once the limited run is gone.

The Speed Shop is the most recent addition to the Flathead family, announced at the start of 2026 as a deliberate shift in direction from the original blend. Where the Flathead is built around a dark Connecticut Broadleaf wrapper and runs full in body, the Speed Shop is designed as a more approachable everyday version of the same concept. The wrapper is an Ecuadorian Habano leaf over a Mexican San Andrés binder, with Nicaraguan and Dominican filler. The box-pressed construction and engine-part naming convention carry over from the original line. Available in three sizes including the Crankshaft, a new vitola introduced with this release. In 24-count boxes. Medium in body. Made at the STG Estelí factory in Nicaragua.

The Bolivar brand has been producing cigars since 1901, named for Simón Bolívar, the Venezuelan general who liberated much of South America from Spanish rule. The connection between the brand and the continent has always been more than symbolic, given that the countries Bolívar freed became the same regions the cigar industry has drawn from for generations. The Bolivar Aniversario makes that connection explicit. Built entirely from South American tobaccos sourced across five countries, including the rarely used growing regions of Argentina and Paraguay alongside more established origins in Ecuador, Brazil, and Colombia, this cigar represents something the brand has never done before. It is a celebration of over 130 years in the industry and a tribute to the land that made the brand worth celebrating. Tobaccos from Argentina and Paraguay almost never appear in premium cigar blends, and their inclusion here is a statement about how seriously Forged Cigar took this release.

Partagas Black Label is the product of a decade of work. Master blender Daniel Nuez and his team spent ten years developing the Medio Tiempo wrapper that defines this cigar, a Cuban-seed leaf harvested only after it has been allowed to age on the stalk roughly fifty percent longer than a standard wrapper leaf. The result is a darker, heavier, and richer leaf that looks like a maduro but is naturally grown to that color without any additional fermentation. Underneath it sits a sun-grown La Vega Especial binder from the Dominican Republic and a filler blend of Nicaraguan Ligero and Dominican Piloto Cubano Ligero, both chosen specifically for the depth and weight they contribute to a blend that was built to be full-bodied from the ground up. Made in the Dominican Republic, the Black Label earned a 90 rating from Cigar Aficionado and was named Best of the Best by Robb Report in 2002. It is the kind of cigar the Partagas name was built on, and it has not changed.

El Titan de Bronze has operated out of Little Havana in Miami since 1995, hand rolling every cigar at their factory on Calle Ocho using Cuban-trained level 9 rollers and traditional entubado construction. The Nicaragua line, which began shipping in March 2026, is the first cigar in the company's thirty-year history to be produced outside the United States, a deliberate step toward broader distribution without dismantling the boutique identity the Miami factory is built on.
The blend uses a Connecticut Broadleaf wrapper over a Mexican binder and all-Nicaraguan filler, produced at the NACSA factory in Estelí. Four sizes, 20-count boxes. Medium to full in body. Made at Nicaraguan American Cigars S.A. in Estelí, Nicaragua.

La Aroma de Cuba is a brand built around José Pepin Garcia's work with the San Andrés wrapper, which he was among the first to establish as a serious premium varietal. The original Mi Amor earned a 95 from Cigar Aficionado and the number two Cigar of the Year in 2011. The Reserva followed as a deliberate step up in intensity from that baseline, using a top-priming San Andrés Oscuro leaf that receives more sunlight on the plant and undergoes an extended fermentation before rolling.
The wrapper is Mexican San Andrés Oscuro over aged Nicaraguan binder and filler from Garcia family farms. Rated 94 by Cigar Aficionado. Six sizes. Medium to full in body. Made at My Father Cigars factory in Estelí, Nicaragua.

The Punch Rare Corojo line turns 25 this year, and the anniversary release marks the first time the brand has been built entirely around the tobacco its name has always promised. The original Rare Corojo uses an Ecuadorian Sumatra wrapper and has never contained Corojo leaf. The 25th Anniversary corrects that, delivering a Honduran puro sourced entirely from Eiroa family farms in the Jamastran Valley: Corojo wrapper, Corojo binder, and filler combining Corojo and Habano. A single size, 6x54 toro, in 10-count boxes. Regular production. Medium to full in body. Made at Fabrica de Puros Aladino S.A. in the Jamastran Valley, Honduras.

VSOP stands for Very Old Superior Pale, a term borrowed from the cognac world used to indicate aged product, and that framing is deliberate here. The cigars were rolled in 2021 and spent over four years resting in STG's aging room before release, a follow-up to the 2024 King of the World, which was a Honduran puro made at the Aladino factory. The VSOP takes a different direction, moving production to STG Estelí and changing the blend entirely.
The wrapper is Ecuadorian Sumatra over a Nicaraguan Habano binder, with filler from Nicaragua and the Dominican Republic. Single size, 6x52 toro, in 16-count boxes. Limited to 2,000 boxes. Medium to full in body. Made at STG Estelí in Nicaragua.

La Flor Dominicana was founded in 1996 in Santiago and made a decision the following year that defined the brand's identity: they purchased their own farm in La Canela in the Dominican Republic and began growing, aging, and processing their own tobacco. That vertical integration is what makes the Ligero Cabinet Oscuro line meaningful. The binder and filler are estate-grown Dominican ligero leaf from La Canela, harvested from the top primings of the plant where the leaf receives the most sunlight and develops the most intensity. The wrapper is an Ecuadorian Sumatra that has been treated to naturally darken and sweeten it, resulting in the Oscuro designation. The Torpedito measures 4.75x50, making it the smallest and quickest expression in the Cabinet Oscuro lineup. Full in body. Made at La Flor Dominicana's factory in Santiago, Dominican Republic.

The Air Bender was named by founder Litto Gomez after his love of kung fu films, and the blend lives up to the reference. The core blend is estate-grown Dominican binder and filler from LFD's La Canela farm, with the filler incorporating Piloto Cubano, Sumatra seed, and Corojo leaf. The Maduro version uses an Ecuadorian Habano Oscuro wrapper rather than the natural Ecuadorian Habano of the original, which is darker, oilier, and adds an additional layer of depth to an already full-bodied blend. Rated 91 by Cigar Aficionado. Full in body. Made at La Flor Dominicana's factory in Santiago, Dominican Republic.
